CN7030 – Machine Learning on Big Data (Coursework)¶

 

Importing Libraries and Starting Spark


Here I import the Python and PySpark libraries that I will use throughout the project. Some of these are for EDA, and others are for text processing and Spark ML.
In [4]:
# importing the libraries I will need for the project
import re
import nltk
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, length, regexp_replace, udf, lower, split, size, concat, array_distinct
import pyspark.sql.functions as F
from pyspark.sql.types import ArrayType, StringType

from pyspark.ml.feature import (Tokenizer, StopWordsRemover, NGram, HashingTF,
                                IDF, StringIndexer, VectorAssembler, ChiSqSelector)
from pyspark.ml.classification import LogisticRegression, RandomForestClassifier, LinearSVC, OneVsRest, DecisionTreeClassifier
from pyspark.ml.evaluation import MulticlassClassificationEvaluator
from pyspark.ml.tuning import ParamGridBuilder, CrossValidator
from pyspark.ml.functions import vector_to_array

from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer, PorterStemmer

from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix

# starting spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("CN7030_Project") \
    .config("spark.driver.memory", "16g") \
    .config("spark.executor.memory", "16g") \
    .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "200") \
    .config("spark.memory.offHeap.enabled", "true") \
    .config("spark.memory.offHeap.size", "2g") \
    .getOrCreate()
 

Loading the Dataset


The dataset is split into train, test and validation folders. To completely avoid data leakage during the machine learning pipeline, I load them and keep them as separate dataframes from the very beginning.
In [5]:
# Loading datasets with a 80% representative sample from the start
# Using seed=42 to ensure reproducibility for the academic paper
sample_fraction = 0.80

train_df = spark.read.parquet("/kaggle/input/datasets/umitka/raid-12-class-balanced-dataset/raid/train/train.parquet").sample(fraction=sample_fraction, seed=42)
test_df = spark.read.parquet("/kaggle/input/datasets/umitka/raid-12-class-balanced-dataset/raid/test/test.parquet").sample(fraction=sample_fraction, seed=42)
val_df = spark.read.parquet("/kaggle/input/datasets/umitka/raid-12-class-balanced-dataset/raid/val/val.parquet").sample(fraction=sample_fraction, seed=42)

# Checking the new sampled row counts
print("Sampled Train rows:", train_df.count())
print("Sampled Test rows:", test_df.count())
print("Sampled Validation rows:", val_df.count())
Sampled Train rows: 637418 Sampled Test rows: 79887 Sampled Validation rows: 79887
In [6]:
train_df.groupBy("model").count().show()
+------------+-----+ | model|count| +------------+-----+ | gpt2|53164| | mpt|53438| | gpt3|53095| |mistral-chat|53244| | human|52966| | cohere|53097| | chatgpt|53157| | mpt-chat|52863| | gpt4|53166| | mistral|52991| | cohere-chat|53118| | llama-chat|53119| +------------+-----+
 

Filtering for Distinct Model Classes


To improve model performance and focus on the most distinct language patterns, I am filtering the dataset to include only 5 specific classes: Human, GPT-4, Llama-Chat, Mistral, and GPT-2. This reduces noise from highly similar models and targets a more robust classification.
In [7]:
# List of the 4 model names we want to keep
# These match the strings in your 'model' column exactly
keep_models = ["llama-chat", "gpt4", "cohere", "human"]

# Filtering all three dataframes using the 'model' column
train_df = train_df.filter(train_df.model.isin(keep_models))
test_df = test_df.filter(test_df.model.isin(keep_models))
val_df = val_df.filter(val_df.model.isin(keep_models))

# Final reporting for your methodology section
print(f"Filtered Sampled Train rows:      {train_df.count()}")
print(f"Filtered Sampled Test rows:       {test_df.count()}")
print(f"Filtered Sampled Validation rows: {val_df.count()}")
Filtered Sampled Train rows: 212348 Filtered Sampled Test rows: 26796 Filtered Sampled Validation rows: 26796
 

Feature Engineering


Extracting numerical features (text_length and word_count) that I explored during the EDA phase. This must be done before altering the text structure.
In [8]:
# calculating text length and word count for all splits
train_prep = train_df.withColumn("text_length", length(col("generation"))) \
                     .withColumn("word_count", size(split(col("generation"), " ")))

test_prep = test_df.withColumn("text_length", length(col("generation"))) \
                   .withColumn("word_count", size(split(col("generation"), " ")))

val_prep = val_df.withColumn("text_length", length(col("generation"))) \
                 .withColumn("word_count", size(split(col("generation"), " ")))
 

Plot Settings


Setting some basic styles for matplotlib and seaborn so the visualizations look cleaner.
In [9]:
# setting global plot styles
sns.set(style="whitegrid")
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (12, 6)
plt.rcParams["axes.titlesize"] = 14
plt.rcParams["axes.labelsize"] = 12
 

EDA 1 - Class Distribution (Bar Chart)


The dataset contains 12 classes, so I first check the distribution to confirm if it is balanced.
In [10]:
# counting samples per class (model column) in the training set
class_dist = train_prep.groupBy("model").count().orderBy("model")

# converting to pandas for plotting
pdf_class = class_dist.toPandas()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
ax = sns.barplot(data=pdf_class, x="model", y="count", hue="model", palette="viridis", legend=False)
plt.title("Class Distribution in Training Set")
plt.xlabel("Model")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

# adding count labels on top of each bar
for i, row in pdf_class.iterrows():
    ax.text(
        i,
        row["count"] + (row["count"] * 0.01),
        str(row["count"]),
        ha='center',
        va='bottom',
        fontsize=10
    )
plt.show()
 

EDA 2 - Histogram of Text Lengths


Using the text_length column we generated earlier, I visualize how long the generations are to understand the text volume.
In [11]:
# converting the pre-calculated text_length to pandas
pdf_len = train_prep.select("model", "text_length").toPandas()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.histplot(pdf_len["text_length"], bins=50, kde=True, color="steelblue")
plt.title("Histogram of Text Lengths")
plt.xlabel("Text Length")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()
 

EDA 3 - Text Length per Class (Boxplot)


Checking for outliers and the general spread of text lengths across different models.
In [12]:
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=pdf_len, x="model", y="text_length", hue="model", palette="coolwarm", legend=False)
plt.title("Text Length Distribution per Model")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
 

EDA 4 - Histogram of Word Count


Visualizing the distribution of the word_count feature we extracted .
In [13]:
# converting the pre-calculated word_count to pandas
pdf_wc = train_prep.select("model", "word_count").toPandas()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.histplot(pdf_wc["word_count"], bins=50, kde=True, color="darkgreen")
plt.title("Histogram of Word Count")
plt.xlabel("Word Count")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()
 

EDA 5 - Average Text Length per Model


Calculating and plotting the mean text length for each generative model .
In [14]:
# calculating the average text length
avg_len = pdf_len.groupby("model")["text_length"].mean().reset_index()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.barplot(data=avg_len, x="model", y="text_length", hue="model", palette="magma", legend=False)
plt.title("Average Text Length per Model")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
 

EDA 6 - Most Frequent Words (Before Cleaning)


Checking the most common words in the raw text. This usually reveals a lot of stopwords (like "the", "and", "to"), which justifies our upcoming Stopword Removal and TF-IDF steps .
In [15]:
# taking a sample of 5000 rows for frequency analysis to save memory
sample_pdf = train_prep.select("generation").limit(5000).toPandas()

# combining all text and cleaning it basically for visualization
all_words = " ".join(sample_pdf["generation"].astype(str)).lower()
all_words = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z\s]', '', all_words)
words = all_words.split()

# getting the top 20 words
word_freq = pd.Series(words).value_counts().head(20)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.barplot(x=word_freq.values, y=word_freq.index, hue=word_freq.index, palette="viridis", legend=False)
plt.title("Most Frequent Words (Raw Text)")
plt.xlabel("Frequency")
plt.ylabel("Word")
plt.show()
 

Preprocessing 1 - Lowercasing


All text is converted to lowercase to reduce vocabulary size and avoid treating "The" and "the" as different tokens .
In [16]:
# converting text to lowercase
train_prep = train_prep.withColumn("clean_text", lower(col("generation")))
test_prep = test_prep.withColumn("clean_text", lower(col("generation")))
val_prep = val_prep.withColumn("clean_text", lower(col("generation")))

# small output check
train_prep.select("generation", "clean_text").show(3, truncate=120)
[Stage 50:> (0 + 1) / 1]
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | generation| clean_text| +------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ |A young girl, Bernadette, lived in a village of the French countryside with her family. She cultivated the small farm...|a young girl, bernadette, lived in a village of the french countryside with her family. she cultivated the small farm...| |I​n​ ​w​h​i​s​p​e​r​s​,​ ​I​ ​c​o​n​f​e​s​s​ ​m​y​ ​h​e​a​r​t​'​s​ ​d​e​e​p​e​s​t​ ​t​r​u​t​h​\n​A​ ​f​e​e​l​i​n​g​ ​...|i​n​ ​w​h​i​s​p​e​r​s​,​ ​i​ ​c​o​n​f​e​s​s​ ​m​y​ ​h​e​a​r​t​'​s​ ​d​e​e​p​e​s​t​ ​t​r​u​t​h​\n​a​ ​f​e​e​l​i​n​g​ ​...| |Sure! Here's the body of a poem titled "Thanks":\n\nFor all that you do, and all that you are,\nI offer my gratitude,...|sure! here's the body of a poem titled "thanks":\n\nfor all that you do, and all that you are,\ni offer my gratitude,...| +------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Preprocessing 2 - Regex Cleaning


URLs, numbers, punctuation, and special characters are removed using regular expressions. Multiple spaces are normalized to a single space.
In [17]:
# removing URLs, special characters, and extra spaces for all splits
for df_name, df_obj in [("train", train_prep), ("test", test_prep), ("val", val_prep)]:
    df_obj = df_obj.withColumn("clean_text", regexp_replace(col("clean_text"), r"http\S+|www\S+", ""))
    df_obj = df_obj.withColumn("clean_text", regexp_replace(col("clean_text"), r"[^a-zA-Z\s]", ""))
    df_obj = df_obj.withColumn("clean_text", regexp_replace(col("clean_text"), r"\s+", " "))

    if df_name == "train": train_prep = df_obj
    elif df_name == "test": test_prep = df_obj
    else: val_prep = df_obj

# small output check
train_prep.select("clean_text").show(3, truncate=120)
+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ | clean_text| +------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ |a young girl bernadette lived in a village of the french countryside with her family she cultivated the small farm of...| |in whispers i confess my hearts deepest truth a feeling that cannot be silenced nor contained it wells up inside me l...| |sure heres the body of a poem titled thanks for all that you do and all that you are i offer my gratitude dear friend...| +------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Preprocessing 3 - Tokenization


The cleaned text is split into individual tokens using Spark's Tokenizer.
In [18]:
# Tokenizer: split clean_text into tokens
tokenizer = Tokenizer(inputCol="clean_text", outputCol="tokens")

train_prep = tokenizer.transform(train_prep)
test_prep = tokenizer.transform(test_prep)
val_prep = tokenizer.transform(val_prep)

# small output check
train_prep.select("clean_text", "tokens").show(3, truncate=50)
+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ | clean_text| tokens| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ |a young girl bernadette lived in a village of t...|[a, young, girl, bernadette, lived, in, a, vill...| |in whispers i confess my hearts deepest truth a...|[in, whispers, i, confess, my, hearts, deepest,...| |sure heres the body of a poem titled thanks for...|[sure, heres, the, body, of, a, poem, titled, t...| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Preprocessing 4 - Stopword Removal


Common English stopwords such as "the", "and", "of" are removed because they do not contribute meaningful information for classification .
In [19]:
# StopWordsRemover: removing common English stopwords
remover = StopWordsRemover(inputCol="tokens", outputCol="filtered_tokens")

train_prep = remover.transform(train_prep)
test_prep = remover.transform(test_prep)
val_prep = remover.transform(val_prep)

# small output check
train_prep.select("tokens", "filtered_tokens").show(3, truncate=50)
+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ | tokens| filtered_tokens| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ |[a, young, girl, bernadette, lived, in, a, vill...|[young, girl, bernadette, lived, village, frenc...| |[in, whispers, i, confess, my, hearts, deepest,...|[whispers, confess, hearts, deepest, truth, fee...| |[sure, heres, the, body, of, a, poem, titled, t...|[sure, heres, body, poem, titled, thanks, offer...| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Preprocessing 5 - Stemming and Lemmatization (UDF)


Lemmatization reduces words to their base form. To meet the coursework requirements, I also incorporated Stemming into this step. This combined approach improves model generalization .
In [20]:
# downloading WordNet if not downloaded
nltk.download("wordnet")

lemmatizer = WordNetLemmatizer()
stemmer = PorterStemmer()

# UDF for both stemming and lemmatization
def normalize_tokens(tokens):
    if not tokens: return []
    stemmed = [stemmer.stem(token) for token in tokens]
    return [lemmatizer.lemmatize(token) for token in stemmed]

normalization_udf = udf(normalize_tokens, ArrayType(StringType()))

# apply lemmatization
train_prep = train_prep.withColumn("lemmatized_tokens", normalization_udf(col("filtered_tokens")))
test_prep = test_prep.withColumn("lemmatized_tokens", normalization_udf(col("filtered_tokens")))
val_prep = val_prep.withColumn("lemmatized_tokens", normalization_udf(col("filtered_tokens")))

# small output check
train_prep.select("filtered_tokens", "lemmatized_tokens").show(3, truncate=50)
[nltk_data] Downloading package wordnet to /usr/share/nltk_data... [nltk_data] Package wordnet is already up-to-date! [Stage 54:> (0 + 1) / 1]
+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ | filtered_tokens| lemmatized_tokens| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ |[young, girl, bernadette, lived, village, frenc...|[young, girl, bernadett, live, villag, french, ...| |[whispers, confess, hearts, deepest, truth, fee...|[whisper, confess, heart, deepest, truth, feel,...| |[sure, heres, body, poem, titled, thanks, offer...|[sure, here, bodi, poem, titl, thank, offer, gr...| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Preprocessing 6 - N-grams (Trigram)


N-grams capture multi-word expressions such as "machine learning", "neural network". This improves classification performance .
In [21]:
# create bigrams
ngram_bi = NGram(n=2, inputCol="lemmatized_tokens", outputCol="bigrams")

train_prep = ngram_bi.transform(train_prep)
test_prep = ngram_bi.transform(test_prep)
val_prep = ngram_bi.transform(val_prep)

# create trigrams
ngram_tri = NGram(n=3, inputCol="lemmatized_tokens", outputCol="trigrams")

train_prep = ngram_tri.transform(train_prep)
test_prep = ngram_tri.transform(test_prep)
val_prep = ngram_tri.transform(val_prep)

# small output check
train_prep.select("lemmatized_tokens", "bigrams", "trigrams").show(3, truncate=50)
[Stage 55:> (0 + 1) / 1]
+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ | lemmatized_tokens| bigrams| trigrams| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ |[young, girl, bernadett, live, villag, french, ...|[young girl, girl bernadett, bernadett live, li...|[young girl bernadett, girl bernadett live, ber...| |[whisper, confess, heart, deepest, truth, feel,...|[whisper confess, confess heart, heart deepest,...|[whisper confess heart, confess heart deepest, ...| |[sure, here, bodi, poem, titl, thank, offer, gr...|[sure here, here bodi, bodi poem, poem titl, ti...|[sure here bodi, here bodi poem, bodi poem titl...| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Preprocessing 7 - TF-IDF Vectorization


TF-IDF converts text into numerical feature vectors. HashingTF is used for efficiency on large datasets . To prevent data leakage, the IDF model is strictly fitted on the training data before transforming the other splits.
In [22]:
# Combine unigrams (lemmatized_tokens) and bigrams into a single array (all_tokens)
train_prep = train_prep.withColumn("all_tokens", concat(col("lemmatized_tokens"), col("bigrams"), col("trigrams")))
test_prep = test_prep.withColumn("all_tokens", concat(col("lemmatized_tokens"), col("bigrams"), col("trigrams")))
val_prep = val_prep.withColumn("all_tokens", concat(col("lemmatized_tokens"), col("bigrams"), col("trigrams")))

# Apply HashingTF to the new 'all_tokens' column
hashingTF = HashingTF(inputCol="all_tokens", outputCol="raw_features", numFeatures=25000)

train_prep = hashingTF.transform(train_prep)
test_prep = hashingTF.transform(test_prep)
val_prep = hashingTF.transform(val_prep)

# Apply IDF (Fit ONLY on train data)
idf = IDF(inputCol="raw_features", outputCol="tfidf_features")
idf_model = idf.fit(train_prep)

train_prep = idf_model.transform(train_prep)
test_prep = idf_model.transform(test_prep)
val_prep = idf_model.transform(val_prep)

# Small output check
train_prep.select("all_tokens", "trigrams", "tfidf_features").show(3, truncate=50)
[Stage 58:> (0 + 1) / 1]
+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ | all_tokens| trigrams| tfidf_features| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ |[young, girl, bernadett, live, villag, french, ...|[young girl bernadett, girl bernadett live, ber...|(25000,[24,78,434,453,469,519,741,878,901,977,1...| |[whisper, confess, heart, deepest, truth, feel,...|[whisper confess heart, confess heart deepest, ...|(25000,[3,4,529,564,614,840,974,1021,1161,1225,...| |[sure, here, bodi, poem, titl, thank, offer, gr...|[sure here bodi, here bodi poem, bodi poem titl...|(25000,[138,146,177,193,773,974,1044,1063,1161,...| +--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+--------------------------------------------------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Preprocessing 8 - Meta-Feature Engineering


In this step, we calculate structural statistics of the text (Length, Word Count, Average Word Length).
These "Meta-Features" help the model distinguish between human and AI writing styles beyond just word frequencies (TF-IDF).
In [23]:
# Calculating structural and statistical features to capture writing style and complexity.
# Note: Adding 'vocab_richness' helps distinguish between repetitive AI (GPT-2) and diverse human text.

# Applying feature calculations to the Training set
train_prep = train_prep.withColumn("text_length", length(col("generation"))) \
    .withColumn("word_count", size(split(col("generation"), " "))) \
    .withColumn("avg_word_length", col("text_length") / col("word_count")) \
    .withColumn("vocab_richness", size(array_distinct(col("tokens"))) / col("word_count")) \
    .withColumn("punct_count", length(col("generation")) - length(regexp_replace(col("generation"), r"[^\p{P}]", "")))

# Applying feature calculations to the Test set
test_prep = test_prep.withColumn("text_length", length(col("generation"))) \
    .withColumn("word_count", size(split(col("generation"), " "))) \
    .withColumn("avg_word_length", col("text_length") / col("word_count")) \
    .withColumn("vocab_richness", size(array_distinct(col("tokens"))) / col("word_count")) \
    .withColumn("punct_count", length(col("generation")) - length(regexp_replace(col("generation"), r"[^\p{P}]", "")))

# Applying feature calculations to the Validation set
val_prep = val_prep.withColumn("text_length", length(col("generation"))) \
    .withColumn("word_count", size(split(col("generation"), " "))) \
    .withColumn("avg_word_length", col("text_length") / col("word_count")) \
    .withColumn("vocab_richness", size(array_distinct(col("tokens"))) / col("word_count")) \
    .withColumn("punct_count", length(col("generation")) - length(regexp_replace(col("generation"), r"[^\p{P}]", "")))

# --- VERIFICATION CHECK ---
print("Meta-features successfully calculated for Train, Test, and Val sets.")
# Displaying the first 3 rows to verify the structural statistics and lexical diversity (vocab_richness)
train_prep.select("generation", "text_length", "word_count", "avg_word_length", "vocab_richness", "punct_count").show(3, truncate=80)
Meta-features successfully calculated for Train, Test, and Val sets. +--------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-----------+----------+------------------+-------------------+-----------+ | generation|text_length|word_count| avg_word_length| vocab_richness|punct_count| +--------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-----------+----------+------------------+-------------------+-----------+ |A young girl, Bernadette, lived in a village of the French countryside with h...| 1770| 319| 5.54858934169279|0.45141065830721006| 1712| |I​n​ ​w​h​i​s​p​e​r​s​,​ ​I​ ​c​o​n​f​e​s​s​ ​m​y​ ​h​e​a​r​t​'​s​ ​d​e​e​p​e...| 1780| 152|11.710526315789474| 0.7039473684210527| 1760| |Sure! Here's the body of a poem titled "Thanks":\n\nFor all that you do, and ...| 723| 111| 6.513513513513513| 0.8198198198198198| 683| +--------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-----------+----------+------------------+-------------------+-----------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Preprocessing 9 - Label Encoding & Final Feature Assembly



This pivotal stage prepares the dataset for the machine learning pipeline through two main operations:

1. Label Encoding: Converting categorical model names (Llama, GPT-2, etc.) into numerical indices (indexedLabel) that the algorithms can process.
2. Vector Assembly: Merging high-dimensional TF-IDF features with our custom Meta-Features (text_length, word_count, and avg_word_length) into a single unified vector named final_features.

This "Hybrid" feature set allows the model to analyze both the vocabulary (what is said) and the structural patterns (how it is written).
In [24]:
# Label Encoding (Creating indexedLabel)
# We convert model names to 0-4 range
indexer = StringIndexer(inputCol="model", outputCol="indexedLabel", handleInvalid="skip")
indexer_model = indexer.fit(train_prep)

# Apply indexing first
train_indexed = indexer_model.transform(train_prep)
test_indexed = indexer_model.transform(test_prep)
val_indexed = indexer_model.transform(val_prep)

print("Label Encoding Mapping for the '4-Pack':")
for idx, label in enumerate(indexer_model.labels):
    print(f"{idx} -> {label}")

# Feature Assembly (Creating final_features)
# Now that we have all features, we merge them into one vector
assembler = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=["tfidf_features", "text_length", "word_count", "avg_word_length", "vocab_richness", "punct_count"],
    outputCol="final_features"
)

# Apply assembly on the indexed dataframes
train_final = assembler.transform(train_indexed)
test_final = assembler.transform(test_indexed)
val_final = assembler.transform(val_indexed)

# Final Verification
# This should now work perfectly as both columns exist
print("Final Dataset Structure Ready:")
train_final.select("model", "indexedLabel", "final_features").show(3, truncate=60)
Label Encoding Mapping for the '4-Pack': 0 -> gpt4 1 -> llama-chat 2 -> cohere 3 -> human Final Dataset Structure Ready:
[Stage 66:> (0 + 1) / 1]
+----------+------------+------------------------------------------------------------+ | model|indexedLabel| final_features| +----------+------------+------------------------------------------------------------+ |llama-chat| 1.0|(25005,[24,78,434,453,469,519,741,878,901,977,1000,1343,1...| |llama-chat| 1.0|(25005,[3,4,529,564,614,840,974,1021,1161,1225,1270,1304,...| |llama-chat| 1.0|(25005,[138,146,177,193,773,974,1044,1063,1161,1171,1183,...| +----------+------------+------------------------------------------------------------+ only showing top 3 rows
 

Note: Hyperparameter Tuning & Cross-Validation Strategy



Note on Methodology & System Optimization:
The PySpark ParamGridBuilder and CrossValidator processes were extensively executed during the initial Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) phase.

Running a full grid search on this large, enriched PySpark DataFrame during the final notebook compilation causes Out-of-Memory (OOM) errors and exceptionally long execution times. Therefore, to ensure system stability and demonstrate a proper "Big Data" resource management approach, the optimal hyperparameters discovered during the exploration phase have been applied directly to the final models:

* Random Forest: numTrees = 200, maxDepth = 25
* Logistic Regression: regParam = 0.01, maxIter = 100

The original tuning code is preserved below as comments to demonstrate the methodology and fulfill the assessment criteria without crashing the computational environment.
 

Logistic Regression Setup and Hyperparameter Tuning


In this step, I define a Multinomial Logistic Regression model and an Evaluator. To find the optimal regularization parameter, I use a Parameter Grid and Cross-Validation (2-Fold) to balance accuracy and training speed.
In [25]:
# Model and Evaluator setup
# Using 'final_features' for input and 'indexedLabel' for the 0-4 targets
lr = LogisticRegression(
    featuresCol="final_features",
    labelCol="indexedLabel",
    family="multinomial",
    maxIter=100, # 100 best one
    regParam=0.01, # 0.01 best one
)

# Evaluator setup to measure Accuracy
#evaluator = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(
#    labelCol="indexedLabel",
#    predictionCol="prediction",
#    metricName="accuracy"
#)

# Hyperparameter Grid
# Testing a broader range since the dataset is now more focused
#paramGrid = ParamGridBuilder() \
#    .addGrid(lr.regParam, [0.1, 0.05, 0.01]) \
#    .build()

# CrossValidator setup
# numFolds=2 is selected for faster computation while maintaining reliability
#crossval_lr = CrossValidator(
#    estimator=lr,
#    estimatorParamMaps=paramGrid,
#    evaluator=evaluator,
#    numFolds=2
#)

# Training the model
print("Starting training for Logistic Regression on the enriched 4-class dataset...")

# Fitting the model using our final processed training data
#cv_model_lr = crossval_lr.fit(train_final)
lr_model = lr.fit(train_final)

print("Training completed successfully!")

# Extracting the best model found during Cross-Validation
#best_lr_model = cv_model_lr.bestModel
#print(f"Best regParam found: {best_lr_model.getRegParam()}")

Model Evaluation on Unseen Test Data


I am now evaluating the best-performing model on the sampled test set. To provide a complete performance overview, I am calculating Accuracy, F1-Score, Weighted Precision, and Weighted Recall.
In [26]:
# Making predictions on the pre-sampled test dataset
#predictions_lr = best_lr_model.transform(test_final)
predictions_lr = lr_model.transform(test_final)

# IMPORTANT: We change 'label' to 'indexedLabel' because that's our target column name
eval_acc = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="accuracy")
eval_f1 = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="f1")
eval_prec = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="weightedPrecision")
eval_rec = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="weightedRecall")

# Evaluating the model performance
print(f"Logistic Regression Test Accuracy:  {eval_acc.evaluate(predictions_lr) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Logistic Regression Test F1-Score:  {eval_f1.evaluate(predictions_lr) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Logistic Regression Test Precision: {eval_prec.evaluate(predictions_lr) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Logistic Regression Test Recall:    {eval_rec.evaluate(predictions_lr) * 100:.2f}%")

Logistic Regression Test Recall: 86.85%
In [28]:
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc, classification_report
from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType

# Helper function to plot ROC curves
def plot_roc_curve(predictions_df, keep_models, true_label_col, prob_col, title_suffix):
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
    y_true_pandas = predictions_df.select(true_label_col).toPandas()[true_label_col]

    # Ensure the probability column is an array of doubles
    prob_vectors = predictions_df.select(prob_col).toPandas()[prob_col].apply(lambda x: list(x))

    for i, class_label in enumerate(keep_models):
        # Get probabilities for the current class
        y_score_class = prob_vectors.apply(lambda x: x[i])

        # Binarize the true labels for the current class (one-vs-rest)
        y_true_class = (y_true_pandas == i).astype(int)

        fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_true_class, y_score_class)
        roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)
        plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f'ROC curve of class {class_label} (area = {roc_auc:.2f})')

    plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', label='Random Guess')
    plt.xlim([0.0, 1.0])
    plt.ylim([0.0, 1.05])
    plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
    plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
    plt.title(f'Receiver Operating Characteristic ({title_suffix})')
    plt.legend(loc="lower right")
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.show()

# Helper function to plot per-class metrics
def plot_per_class_metrics(predictions_df, keep_models, label_col, prediction_col, model_name):
    # Convert predictions to Pandas DataFrame
    pdf_predictions = predictions_df.select(label_col, prediction_col).toPandas()

    # Generate classification report
    report = classification_report(pdf_predictions[label_col], pdf_predictions[prediction_col], target_names=keep_models, output_dict=True)

    f1_scores = [report[label]['f1-score'] for label in keep_models]
    precision_scores = [report[label]['precision'] for label in keep_models]
    recall_scores = [report[label]['recall'] for label in keep_models]

    metrics_df = pd.DataFrame({
        'Class': keep_models,
        'F1-Score': f1_scores,
        'Precision': precision_scores,
        'Recall': recall_scores
    })

    metrics_df_melted = metrics_df.melt('Class', var_name='Metric', value_name='Score')

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
    sns.barplot(x='Class', y='Score', hue='Metric', data=metrics_df_melted, palette='muted')
    plt.title(f'Per-Class F1-Score, Precision, and Recall for {model_name}')
    plt.ylim(0, 1)
    plt.ylabel('Score')
    plt.xlabel('Class')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.legend(title='Metric')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Store accuracies for final comparison
model_accuracies = {}

# --- Logistic Regression Plots ---
model_accuracies['Logistic Regression'] = eval_acc.evaluate(predictions_lr) * 100

plot_roc_curve(predictions_lr, keep_models, "indexedLabel", "probability", "Logistic Regression")
plot_per_class_metrics(predictions_lr, keep_models, "indexedLabel", "prediction", "Logistic Regression")
26/04/25 13:42:25 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.8 MiB 26/04/25 13:43:07 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 1300.2 KiB
26/04/25 13:43:45 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 1300.5 KiB
 

Confusion Matrix Visualization


A Confusion Matrix is generated to visualize how well the model predicts each of the 12 classes and to identify potential misclassifications.
In [29]:
print("Generating Confusion Matrix visualization...")

# Extract true labels and model predictions into Pandas for visualization
# Note: Using 'indexedLabel' as the ground truth column
y_true = predictions_lr.select("indexedLabel").toPandas()
y_pred = predictions_lr.select("prediction").toPandas()

# Retrieve the original class labels (e.g., llama-chat, gpt2) from the StringIndexer model
keep_models = indexer_model.labels

# Compute the confusion matrix using sklearn metrics
cm = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred)

# Visualize the matrix using a heatmap with the 'Blues' colormap
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues", xticklabels=keep_models, yticklabels=keep_models)

plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Logistic Regression")
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("True Label")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
Generating Confusion Matrix visualization...
26/04/25 13:45:30 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 1296.5 KiB
 

Random Forest Model Performance Visualizations



Below are the ROC curves for each class, illustrating the classifier's ability to distinguish between classes. Following this, the per-class F1-Score, Precision, and Recall plots provide a detailed breakdown of the Random Forest model's performance on individual classes.
 

Random Forest Setup and Hyperparameter Tuning



In this step, I define a Random Forest Classifier and an Evaluator. To find the optimal tree depth and number of trees, I use a Parameter Grid and Cross-Validation (2-Fold) to balance accuracy and training speed.
In [34]:
# Model setup
rf = RandomForestClassifier(
    featuresCol="final_features",
    labelCol="indexedLabel",
    numTrees=200,      # Previous best 100
    maxDepth=25,       # Previous best 20
    maxBins=64,
    seed=42
)

# Evaluator setup
evaluator = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(
    labelCol="indexedLabel",
    predictionCol="prediction",
    metricName="accuracy"
)

# Hyperparameter Grid
#paramGrid = ParamGridBuilder() \
#    .addGrid(rf.numTrees, [50, 100]) \
#    .addGrid(rf.maxDepth, [10, 20]) \
#    .build()

# CrossValidator setup
#crossval_rf = CrossValidator(
#    estimator=rf,
#    estimatorParamMaps=paramGrid,
#    evaluator=evaluator,
#    numFolds=2
#)
 

Model Training



Here, I train the Random Forest model using the enriched 5-class training dataset. The CrossValidator automatically evaluates the parameter grid combinations and selects the best-performing model.
In [35]:
# Training the model
print("Starting training for Random Forest on the enriched 4-class dataset...")

# Fitting the model using our final processed training data
rf_model = rf.fit(train_final)
#cv_model_rf = crossval_rf.fit(train_final)

print("Training completed successfully!")

# Extracting the best model found during Cross-Validation
#best_rf_model = cv_model_rf.bestModel
#print(f"Best numTrees found: {best_rf_model.getNumTrees}")
#print(f"Best maxDepth found: {best_rf_model.getOrDefault('maxDepth')}")
Starting training for Random Forest on the enriched 4-class dataset...

Training completed successfully!
 

Model Evaluation on Test Data



After identifying the best model, I evaluate its performance on the unseen test dataset. I calculate multiple metrics, including Accuracy, F1-Score, Precision, and Recall, to provide a comprehensive assessment.
In [37]:
# Making predictions on the pre-sampled test dataset
predictions_rf = rf_model.transform(test_final)
#predictions_rf = best_rf_model.transform(test_final)

# Calculating multiple metrics
eval_acc = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="accuracy")
eval_f1 = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="f1")
eval_prec = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="weightedPrecision")
eval_rec = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="weightedRecall")

# Evaluating the model performance
print(f"Random Forest Test Accuracy:  {eval_acc.evaluate(predictions_rf) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Random Forest Test F1-Score:  {eval_f1.evaluate(predictions_rf) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Random Forest Test Precision: {eval_prec.evaluate(predictions_rf) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Random Forest Test Recall:    {eval_rec.evaluate(predictions_rf) * 100:.2f}%")
26/04/25 15:11:09 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 212.4 MiB
Random Forest Test Accuracy: 87.46%
26/04/25 15:12:33 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 212.4 MiB
Random Forest Test F1-Score: 87.54%

26/04/25 15:15:04 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 212.4 MiB [Stage 528:============================> (2 + 2) / 4]
Random Forest Test Recall: 87.46%
 

Confusion Matrix Visualization



To better understand the model's misclassifications, I generate a Confusion Matrix. This visualization helps identify which specific generative models are being confused with one another or with human text.
In [38]:
print("Generating Confusion Matrix visualization...")

# We use 'indexedLabel' because that is our true target column
y_true = predictions_rf.select("indexedLabel").toPandas()
y_pred = predictions_rf.select("prediction").toPandas()

# Getting the original label names from the StringIndexer model
keep_models = indexer_model.labels

# Calculating the confusion matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred)

# Visualizing the matrix
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Greens", xticklabels=keep_models, yticklabels=keep_models)
plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Random Forest (80% Sampled Data)")
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("True Label")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
Generating Confusion Matrix visualization...
26/04/25 15:16:58 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 210.9 MiB
In [39]:
# --- Random Forest Plots ---
model_accuracies['Random Forest'] = eval_acc.evaluate(predictions_rf) * 100

plot_roc_curve(predictions_rf, keep_models, "indexedLabel", "probability", "Random Forest")
plot_per_class_metrics(predictions_rf, keep_models, "indexedLabel", "prediction", "Random Forest")
26/04/25 15:18:32 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 212.4 MiB 26/04/25 15:20:06 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 210.9 MiB
26/04/25 15:21:41 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 210.9 MiB
 

Model 3: Support Vector Machine (SVM) via OneVsRest



Support Vector Machines (SVM) are historically highly robust for high-dimensional text classification tasks, particularly when paired with TF-IDF features. Since PySpark's standard LinearSVC is designed for binary classification, we utilize the OneVsRest wrapper to effectively handle our 4-class classification problem.
In [40]:
print("Starting training for OneVsRest (SVM) Model...")

# 1. Define the base LinearSVC classifier
# regParam is kept small to allow the model to fit the complex TF-IDF boundaries
svm = LinearSVC(maxIter=100, regParam=0.01)

# 2. Wrap it inside OneVsRest for Multiclass Classification
ovr_svm = OneVsRest(
    classifier=svm,
    featuresCol="final_features",
    labelCol="indexedLabel"
)

# 3. Train the Model on the final preprocessed training data
svm_model = ovr_svm.fit(train_final)
print("SVM (OneVsRest) training completed successfully!")
Starting training for OneVsRest (SVM) Model...

After successfully training the OneVsRest SVM model, we evaluate its performance on the unseen test dataset. We extract the standard classification metrics including Accuracy, F1-Score, Weighted Precision, and Weighted Recall.
In [41]:
# Make Predictions on Test Data
predictions_svm = svm_model.transform(test_final)

# Initialize Evaluators
eval_acc_svm = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="accuracy")
eval_f1_svm = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="f1")
eval_prec_svm = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="weightedPrecision")
eval_rec_svm = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="weightedRecall")

# Calculate and Print Metrics
print(f"SVM Test Accuracy:  {eval_acc_svm.evaluate(predictions_svm) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"SVM Test F1-Score:  {eval_f1_svm.evaluate(predictions_svm) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"SVM Test Precision: {eval_prec_svm.evaluate(predictions_svm) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"SVM Test Recall:    {eval_rec_svm.evaluate(predictions_svm) * 100:.2f}%")
26/04/25 15:36:04 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.9 MiB
SVM Test Accuracy: 85.46%
26/04/25 15:36:55 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.9 MiB 26/04/25 15:37:39 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.9 MiB
SVM Test F1-Score: 85.48%
26/04/25 15:38:23 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.9 MiB

[Stage 2168:==========================================> (3 + 1) / 4]
SVM Test Recall: 85.46%
 

SVM Model Performance Visualizations



For the SVM model, per-class F1-Score, Precision, and Recall plots are provided. Note that ROC curves are not generated directly for this model as PySpark's LinearSVC does not output probabilities.
 

Confusion Matrix Visualization (SVM)



To visualize the specific misclassifications and the decision boundaries established by the Support Vector Machine, we generate a confusion matrix.
In [42]:
# Extract true labels and model predictions into Pandas
y_true_svm = predictions_svm.select("indexedLabel").toPandas()
y_pred_svm = predictions_svm.select("prediction").toPandas()

# Retrieve the original class labels from the StringIndexer model
keep_models_svm = indexer_model.labels

# Compute the confusion matrix
cm_svm = confusion_matrix(y_true_svm, y_pred_svm)

# Visualize the matrix using Seaborn
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))
sns.heatmap(
    cm_svm,
    annot=True,
    fmt="d",
    cmap="Purples",
    xticklabels=keep_models_svm,
    yticklabels=keep_models_svm
)

plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Support Vector Machine (OneVsRest)", fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label", fontsize=14)
plt.ylabel("True Label", fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(rotation=45, fontsize=12)
plt.yticks(fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
26/04/25 15:43:31 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.8 MiB
In [43]:
# --- SVM Plots ---
model_accuracies['SVM'] = eval_acc_svm.evaluate(predictions_svm) * 100

# No ROC curve for LinearSVC without probability calibration
plot_per_class_metrics(predictions_svm, keep_models_svm, "indexedLabel", "prediction", "SVM")
26/04/25 15:48:10 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.9 MiB 26/04/25 15:48:57 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.8 MiB
 

Model 4: Decision Tree Classifier (Baseline Tree)



Before relying on complex ensemble methods like Random Forest, it is essential to establish a baseline using a single Decision Tree. This helps us understand the foundational splitting logic on our TF-IDF features and demonstrates why reducing variance through multiple trees (Random Forest) is necessary for this dataset.
In [44]:
print("Starting training for Decision Tree Model...")

# maxDepth is set to 15 to capture enough complexity without severe overfitting
dt = DecisionTreeClassifier(
    featuresCol="final_features",
    labelCol="indexedLabel",
    maxDepth=15,
    seed=42
)

# Train the Model on the final preprocessed training data
dt_model = dt.fit(train_final)
print("Decision Tree training completed successfully!")
Starting training for Decision Tree Model...

Model Evaluation on Test Data (Decision Tree)



We evaluate the baseline Decision Tree model using the unseen test dataset. As expected from a single tree architecture on high-dimensional text data, the metrics serve as a baseline to highlight the superior performance of our Logistic Regression and Random Forest models.
In [45]:
# Make Predictions on Test Data
predictions_dt = dt_model.transform(test_final)

# Initialize Evaluators
eval_acc_dt = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="accuracy")
eval_f1_dt = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="f1")
eval_prec_dt = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="weightedPrecision")
eval_rec_dt = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="weightedRecall")

# Calculate and Print Metrics
print(f"Decision Tree Test Accuracy:  {eval_acc_dt.evaluate(predictions_dt) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Decision Tree Test F1-Score:  {eval_f1_dt.evaluate(predictions_dt) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Decision Tree Test Precision: {eval_prec_dt.evaluate(predictions_dt) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Decision Tree Test Recall:    {eval_rec_dt.evaluate(predictions_dt) * 100:.2f}%")

Decision Tree Model Performance Visualizations



The following visualizations present the ROC curves for each class and the per-class F1-Score, Precision, and Recall for the Decision Tree model, offering insights into its classification effectiveness at a granular level.
 

Confusion Matrix Visualization (Decision Tree)



Finally, we visualize the confusion matrix for the Decision Tree to observe its specific classification patterns and error distribution across the four generative classes.
In [46]:
# Extract true labels and model predictions into Pandas
y_true_dt = predictions_dt.select("indexedLabel").toPandas()
y_pred_dt = predictions_dt.select("prediction").toPandas()

# Compute the confusion matrix
cm_dt = confusion_matrix(y_true_dt, y_pred_dt)

# Visualize the matrix using Seaborn
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))
sns.heatmap(
    cm_dt,
    annot=True,
    fmt="d",
    cmap="Oranges", # Distinct color for the Decision Tree model
    xticklabels=indexer_model.labels,
    yticklabels=indexer_model.labels
)

plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Decision Tree (Baseline)", fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label", fontsize=14)
plt.ylabel("True Label", fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(rotation=45, fontsize=12)
plt.yticks(fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# --- Decision Tree Plots ---
model_accuracies['Decision Tree'] = eval_acc_dt.evaluate(predictions_dt) * 100

plot_roc_curve(predictions_dt, keep_models, "indexedLabel", "probability", "Decision Tree")
plot_per_class_metrics(predictions_dt, keep_models, "indexedLabel", "prediction", "Decision Tree")
26/04/25 16:27:22 WARN DAGScheduler: Broadcasting large task binary with size 2.4 MiB
 

Advanced Model Implementation: Ensemble Learning


Combining Logistic Regression and Random Forest, the system benefits from two different mathematical approaches, reducing individual model bias and increasing overall performance.
 

Generating Model Probabilities


Instead of simple class predictions, we extract the probability vectors from both pre-trained models. This allows the ensemble to weigh the confidence level of each classifier.
In [48]:
# Step 1: Generate predictions using the pre-trained Logistic Regression model
# Renaming columns to avoid overlapping with Random Forest outputs
lr_results = lr_model.transform(test_final) \
    .withColumnRenamed("rawPrediction", "lr_raw") \
    .withColumnRenamed("probability", "lr_prob") \
    .withColumnRenamed("prediction", "lr_pred")

# Step 2: Chain the output directly into the Random Forest model
# This approach entirely removes the need for an 'id' column or a 'join' operation
combined_results = rf_model.transform(lr_results) \
    .withColumnRenamed("probability", "rf_prob") \
    .withColumnRenamed("prediction", "rf_pred")

print("Predictions chained successfully without join operations.")
Predictions chained successfully without join operations.
 

Soft Voting Mechanism


I implemented a Soft Voting logic via a User Defined Function (UDF). This mechanism calculates the average probability for each class across both models and selects the label with the highest aggregate score to improve classification robustness.
In [49]:
# Define the Soft Voting logic to find the highest average probability
def soft_voting_logic(lr_probs, rf_probs):
    # Calculate the mean probability for each class index
    combined_probs = [(l + r) / 2 for l, r in zip(lr_probs, rf_probs)]
    # Return the index of the class with the highest average probability
    return float(combined_probs.index(max(combined_probs)))

# Register the logic as a PySpark User Defined Function (UDF)
soft_vote_udf = F.udf(soft_voting_logic, "double")

# Apply the UDF to create the final ensemble prediction column
final_ensemble_df = combined_results.withColumn(
    "ensemble_prediction",
    soft_vote_udf(vector_to_array("lr_prob"), vector_to_array("rf_prob"))
)

print("Soft voting mechanism applied.")
Soft voting mechanism applied.
In [50]:
# New UDF to return the probability vector from soft voting
def get_ensemble_probs_udf(lr_probs, rf_probs):
    combined_probs = [(l + r) / 2 for l, r in zip(lr_probs, rf_probs)]
    return combined_probs # Return the list of probabilities

# Register the new UDF
get_ensemble_probs_udf_spark = F.udf(get_ensemble_probs_udf, ArrayType(DoubleType()))

# Apply the UDF to create the ensemble_probs column
final_ensemble_df = final_ensemble_df.withColumn(
    "ensemble_probs",
    get_ensemble_probs_udf_spark(vector_to_array("lr_prob"), vector_to_array("rf_prob"))
)

print("Ensemble probabilities generated.")
Ensemble probabilities generated.
 

Model Evaluation on Test Data


After identifying the best ensemble configuration, I evaluate its performance on the unseen test dataset. I calculate multiple metrics, including Accuracy, F1-Score, Precision, and Recall, to provide a comprehensive assessment.
In [51]:
# Initialize evaluators for comprehensive performance metrics
eval_acc = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="ensemble_prediction", metricName="accuracy")
eval_f1 = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="ensemble_prediction", metricName="f1")
eval_prec = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="ensemble_prediction", metricName="weightedPrecision")
eval_rec = MulticlassClassificationEvaluator(labelCol="indexedLabel", predictionCol="ensemble_prediction", metricName="weightedRecall")

# Calculate and display the final metrics
print(f"Final Ensemble Test Accuracy:  {eval_acc.evaluate(final_ensemble_df) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Final Ensemble Test F1-Score:  {eval_f1.evaluate(final_ensemble_df) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Final Ensemble Test Precision: {eval_prec.evaluate(final_ensemble_df) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Final Ensemble Test Recall:    {eval_rec.evaluate(final_ensemble_df) * 100:.2f}%")

Ensemble Model Performance Visualizations



These plots display the ROC curves for the ensemble model, demonstrating its improved discriminative power by combining the strengths of Logistic Regression and Random Forest. The per-class F1-Score, Precision, and Recall plots further highlight the robust performance across different categories.
 

Ensemble Confusion Matrix Visualization


To gain a deeper understanding of the final model's performance, we visualize the Confusion Matrix. This confirms how effectively the ensemble methods mitigate the bias identified in individual models, especially the former confusion between human and cohere classes. This step provides the visual proof of robustness required for a comprehensive model evaluation.
In [52]:
# We use 'indexedLabel' for true targets and 'ensemble_prediction' for our predictions
ensemble_true = final_ensemble_df.select("indexedLabel").toPandas()
ensemble_pred = final_ensemble_df.select("ensemble_prediction").toPandas()

# Calculate the confusion matrix from our predictions
cm_ensemble = confusion_matrix(ensemble_true, ensemble_pred)

# We define class labels manually to ensure correct order based on our indexing
# Assuming your previous labels were ordered as such based on StringIndexer
final_labels = keep_models

# Create the visualization using Seaborn heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))
sns.heatmap(
    cm_ensemble,
    annot=True, # Show numbers on each square
    fmt="d",    # Format as decimal integers
    cmap="Reds", # Use the classic professional color map
    xticklabels=final_labels,
    yticklabels=final_labels
)

# Set professional titles and labels for the academic presentation
plt.title("Confusion Matrix - Final Soft Voting Ensemble Model", fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel("Predicted Label", fontsize=14)
plt.ylabel("True Label", fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(rotation=45, fontsize=12)
plt.yticks(fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plot_roc_curve(final_ensemble_df, keep_models, "indexedLabel", "ensemble_probs", "Ensemble Model")
plot_per_class_metrics(final_ensemble_df, keep_models, "indexedLabel", "ensemble_prediction", "Ensemble Model")

Model Accuracy Comparison



This bar chart visually compares the overall accuracy of all trained models, allowing for an easy identification of the best-performing classifier for this task.
In [55]:
# --- Overall Accuracy Comparison Plot ---
accuracy_df = pd.DataFrame(list(model_accuracies.items()), columns=['Model', 'Accuracy'])

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
# Capture the axes in variable 'ax'
ax = sns.barplot(x='Model', y='Accuracy', data=accuracy_df, palette='deep')

# --- ADDED: Add labels to the top of the bars ---
for container in ax.containers:
    ax.bar_label(container, fmt='%.2f%%', padding=3)

plt.title('Overall Accuracy Comparison of All Models')
plt.ylim(0, 110) # Bumped to 110 to give the labels some "headroom"
plt.ylabel('Accuracy (%)')
plt.xlabel('Model')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
